.\outputs\audits\I-redact-001\redaction_fixture.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template. Sponsor / conflict-of-interest review per source.\nPrompt-injection sanitization enabled. Retrieved 2026-06-10.\nExpected tier distribution: T3 35-70%, T1 10-30%, T2 5-25%, T6 5-25%, T4 0-15%, T5 0-10%, T7 0-5%. Actual distribution: T1=2%, T3=1%, T4=5%, T5=0%, T6=4%, T7=5%, UNKNOWN=82%.\nCorpus adequacy: decision=proceed, 8/8 thresholds met.\nCompleteness checklist: 6/6 topics covered.\n\n## Capability disclosures\nQuantified trade-off analysis was ENABLED but did not contribute to this report (no verified quantified output); 249 sourced numbers were extracted but not modeled into a verified quantified comparison.\n\n## Contradiction disclosures\nThe contradiction detector flagged 2 numeric disagreements across the evidence pool. Most are extraction artifacts produced by grouping different measured endpoints, units, sub-populations, time windows, or comparators under the same subject/predicate label. The detector does NOT adjudicate by endpoint, population, timepoint, or source tier; raw detector output is available in `contradictions.json`. Per-flag enumeration (PT08 disclosure):\n\n- unknown / accuracy: cited values range 43.0 to 100.0 % (source tiers: UNKNOWN).\n- unknown / ttr: cited values range 6.9 to 99.5 % (source tiers: T4, UNKNOWN, T1, T6).\n\nClaims made in the body of this report are individually bound to their cited evidence IDs via the strict-verify gate, so the reader can trace any specific numeric discrepancy to its source regardless of detector granularity.\n\n\n## Bibliography\n[1] sae_j3016_automation_levels — https://www.sae.org/standards/content/j3016_202104/ (tier T1)\n[2] unece_alks_regulation_framework — https://unece.org/transport/documents/2021/03/standards/un-regulation-no-157-automated-lane-keeping-systems-alks (tier T1)\n[3] nhtsa_ads_policy_framework — https://www.nhtsa.gov/laws-regulations/standing-general-order-crash-reporting (tier T1)\n[4] product_liability_doctrine — https://www.ali.org/publications/restatement-law-third/torts-third (tier T1)\n[5] adas_crash_case_law — https://www.courtlistener.com/docket/59932667/benavides-v-tesla-inc/ (tier T1)\n[6] proposed_regulatory_guidelines — https://www.ntsb.gov/Advocacy/mwl/Pages/mwl-22-23/mwl-hs.aspx (tier T1)\n[7] Analysis of Advanced Driver-Assistance Systems for Safe and ... - PMC — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11479187/ (tier T1)\n[8] Classifying Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) Activation ... — https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12526685/ (tier T4)\n[9] [PDF] Road Safety Thematic Report Advanced driver assistance systems — https://road-safety.transport.ec.europa.eu/document/download/d786ded1-66ce-4571-95e5-1ff38fff5c74_en?filename=Road_Safety_Thematic_Report_ADAS_2021.pdf (tier T3)\n[10] [PDF] Cause-and-Effect Analysis of ADAS - arXiv — https://arxiv.org/pdf/2208.00249 (tier T4)\n[11] Human involvement in autonomous decision-making systems. However, it will also lead to a 0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force. For each one-unit increase in AI, the employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001% due to the replacement effect. This indicates that AI increases the demand for high- and medium-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour. This can be considered an improvement in the labour force employment structure as it in\\n\\n[...]\\n\\nf Cong and Yu. We must preserve the [#ev:ev_004:5700-6500] marker exactly. We cannot introduce new specifics not in the span. The span does mention: \"a 0.063% increase in the high-skilled labour force\", \"a 0.001% increase in the medium-skilled labour force\", \"a 0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force\", \"employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001%\", and \"AI increases the demand for high- and medium-skilled labour while reducing the demand for low-skilled labour.\" It does not mention China, provincial panel data, 2010-2019, N=310. So we must drop those specifics. Also, the original sentence says \"0.001% decrease in low-skilled employment\" but the span says \"0.001-unit decrease in the growth of the low-skilled labour force\" and \"employment share of lower-skilled workers will decrease by 0.001%\". So we can say a decrease in low-skilled employment share or something. The span also says \"a 0.001% increase in the medium-skilled labour force\" which the original omitted. 0.94.[1] Colorectal cancer incidence was the outcome of interest in all included cohorts.[1] The more than 1.7 million participants contributed to the statistical power of the analysis.[1]\n\n### Pathogenic bacteria and their genotoxic metabolites in colorectal carcinogenesis\n\nPathogen: Escherichia coli.[3] Mechanism: alkylate DNA on adenine residues4,5 and induces double-strand breaks.[3] Mutational signature: distinct mutational signature.[3] Toxic metabolite or virulence factor: colibactin.[3] This bacterium produces the genotoxic metabolite colibactin.[3] Colibactin is a virulence factor that damages host DNA.[3] The mechanism of colibactin's genotoxicity involves DNA alkylation.[3] Specifically, colibactin alkylates DNA on adenine residues4,5.[3] Following alkylation, colibactin induces double-strand breaks.[3] The induction of double-strand breaks is a direct outcome of colibactin's activity.[3] As a result of this DNA damage, a distinct mutational signature is generated.[3] This mutational signature is characteristic of colibactin exposure.[3] The distinct mutational signature can be detected in colorectal carcinogenesis.[3] Colibactin is a genotoxin that targets DNA.[3] The genotoxic metabolite colibactin is directly responsible for the DNA damage.[3] Ultimately, the mechanism of colibactin involves alkylating DNA on adenine residues4,5 and inducing double-strand breaks, leaving a distinct mutational signature.[3]\n\n### Probiotic contraindications and at-risk populations\n\nContract-bound content for probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Safety\n\nAmong 125 patients with nonblood *Saccharomyces* culture findings, 24 (19%) had used the probiotic, versus 2% (3/123) of controls (OR 10, 95% CI 3–32).[4] Consequently, *S. cerevisiae* var. *boulardii* probiotics are not recommended for patients with indwelling catheters, those who are immunocompromised, or the critically ill.[4] In one trial, a multispecies probiotic preparation administered to patients with severe acute pancreatitis was associated with increased mortality. *Escherichia coli* carrying the pks pathogenicity island produces colibactin, a genotoxin that alkylates DNA on adenine residues and induces double-strand breaks.[4].[3] Cyclomodulin-producing *E. coli* were present in biopsies from 70% of colorectal cancer patients compared with only 3% of healthy controls.[5] Lipopolysaccharide (LPS) from Gram-negative bacteria such as *Fusobacterium* and *Bacteroides* activates NF-κB signaling, driving inflammation and colorectal cancer metastasis.[6].[7] A retrospective registry study across five Finnish university hospitals from 2009 to 2018 identified 46 patients with *Saccharomyces* fungemia; at least 20 (43%) were using *Saccharomyces boulardii* probiotic, and compared with a control group with bacteremia or candidemia, the odds ratio for use of an *S. boulardii* probiotic was 14 (95% CI 4–44).[4] Human intestinal organoids were exposed to genotoxic pks+ *E. coli* by repeated luminal injection over five months; whole-genome sequencing of clonal organoids before and after this exposure revealed a distinct mutational signature that was absent from organoids injected with isogenic pks-mutant bacteria.[3] The same mutational signature was detected in a subset of 5,876 human cancer genomes from two independent cohorts, predominantly in colorectal cancer.[3] Butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid, stimulates proliferation at low concentrations but suppresses it at high concentrations.[6]\n\n### Dose Response\n\nFor total dietary fiber, each 10 g/day increment was associated with a summary relative risk (RR) of 0.90 (95% CI 0.86 to 0.94) across 16 studies including 14,514 cases among 1,985,552 participants, with no significant heterogeneity (I²=0%).[1] In contrast, fruit fiber intake was not significantly associated with CRC risk (RR 0.93, 95% CI 0.82 to 1.05 per 10 g/day), and vegetable fiber showed no association (RR 0.98, 95% CI 0.91 to 1.06 per 10 g/day).[1] Similarly, a review of harnessing gut microbiota for CRC therapy highlights the potential of microbiome modulators but emphasizes the need for rigorous validation and biomarker development before clinical translation.[8] A systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 prospective studies examined the association between dietary fiber and whole grain intake and colorectal cancer (CRC) risk[1] Cereal fiber demonstrated a similar protective dose-response: each 10 g/day increase yielded an RR of 0.90 (0.83 to 0.97) based on 8 studies with 9,487 cases among 1,471,756 participants.[1] Whole grain intake also exhibited a significant inverse dose-response: an increment of three servings daily (90 g/day) was associated with an RR of 0.83 (0.78 to 0.89) across 6 studies with 7,941 cases among 774,806 participants.[1]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nA meta-analysis across solid tumors indicated that combining FMT with immune checkpoint inhibitors improved pooled objective response rates to 43.1% compared to 36.9% with immunotherapy alone.[9] Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) has shown potential to augment therapies for colorectal cancer.[9] In a study, patients with refractory, MSS metastatic CRC receiving tislelizumab and fruquintinib achieved a median progression-free survival of 9.6 months and median overall survival of 13.7 months, with an objective response rate of 20% and a disease control rate of 95%.[9] Probiotic modulation with CBM588 (Clostridium butyricum) combined with cabozantinib and nivolumab significantly increased response rates (74% vs 20%, P=0.01) in a trial of metastatic renal cell carcinoma.[9]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The evidence base is heavily skewed, with only 17% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and 62% falling into an unknown tier, which undermines the reliability of the evidence hierarchy. Three high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree sharply on the effect on ttr (relative difference 12275.0%), on ttr at 0.5 mg (25.0% difference), and on weight loss (212.5% difference), indicating substantial inconsistency in key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, potentially omitting earlier foundational research and limiting the temporal scope of the analysis.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 feb9fd7172651bfc...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 21}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 375 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 365 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Efficacy.** Population: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1]\n- **Mechanism.** Complication type: hardware complications.[2]\n- **Long-term Outcomes.** The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3]\n- **Safety.** The FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6]\n- **Population Subgroups.** The choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10]\n\n### Efficacy\n\nPopulation: patients with advanced Parkinson's disease and severe motor symptoms.[1] Intervention: this treatment plus medication.[1] Comparator: medication alone.[1] Primary endpoint: the changes from baseline to six months in the quality of life, as assessed by the Parkinson's Disease Questionnaire (PDQ-39), and the severity of symptoms without medication, according to the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, part III (UPDRS-III).[1] Timepoint: six months.[1] Study design: randomized-pairs trial.[1]\n\n### Mechanism\n\nComplication type: hardware complications.[2] The Movement Disorder Society-sponsored Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale, known as the MDS-UPDRS, structures the clinical assessment of Parkinson's disease into four distinct and complementary parts.[3] Whereas Part I addresses the non-motor domain, Part II shifts the focus to motor aspects of daily function.[3] However, Part IV specifically records motor complications, distinguishing them from the core motor examination.[3] The combined clinimetric results of this study support the validity of the MDS-UPDRS for rating PD.[3] The primary source identifies hardware complications as a distinct category of DBS-related adverse events.[2] In contrast, the source does not offer guidance on daily life adjustments or support strategies for patients experiencing hardware complications.[2]\n\n### DBS device contraindications and dopaminergic-withdrawal dosing safety\n\nContract-bound content for dbs_device_mri_safety did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[4]\n\n### Long-term Outcomes\n\nThe Movement Disorder Society-sponsored revision of the Unified Parkinson's Disease Rating Scale (MDS-UPDRS), validated in a study of 877 patients from 39 sites, demonstrated high internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha = 0.79–0.93 across parts) and strong correlation with the original UPDRS (ρ = 0.96), providing a comprehensive framework for tracking these changes across four parts: non-motor experiences of daily living, motor experiences of daily living, motor examination, and motor complications.[3] In advanced stages, symptoms are severe and daily life usually requires full-time support.[5]\n\n### Safety\n\nThe FDA labeling for carbidopa-levodopa explicitly warns that patients should be observed carefully when the dosage is reduced abruptly or discontinued, especially if they are receiving neuroleptics.[6] DBS hardware complications are relatively common; in a prospective series of 81 consecutive patients with PD undergoing subthalamic nucleus (STN) DBS followed for a mean of 17 months, hardware complications occurred in 26.2% of patients (atom_009).[2] Specific complications included infections requiring system removal in 2.5% (atom_010) and implantable pulse generator (IPG) removal in 3.7% (atom_011), misplaced leads in 12.5% (atom_012), and lead migration, fracture, or IPG malfunction.[2] A review of the FDA MAUDE database over 10 years identified 221 unique adverse events, with infection being the most common (16.2%) (atom_013) and lead migration accounting for 8.6% (atom_014).[7] Over a third (40%) of all devices with adverse events required return to the operating room for explant or revision (atom_003, atom_004).[7] A review of postoperative psychosis after DBS reported a lifetime incidence of psychosis of 28.3% (atom_018) in PD patients following DBS, underscoring the need for vigilance.[8] Additionally, implantable pulse generator expiration can precipitate a movement disorder emergency with severe parkinsonian worsening or akinetic crisis, necessitating urgent medical evaluation.[9] Abrupt reduction or discontinuation of carbidopa-levodopa has been associated with a symptom complex resembling neuroleptic malignant syndrome (NMS), characterized by hyperthermia, muscle rigidity, altered consciousness, and autonomic dysfunction.[6] Psychosis can emerge after STN DBS, as illustrated by clinical cases where patients developed paranoid ideation, hallucinations, and delusions within months of surgery, requiring antipsychotic treatment.[8] Patients taking dopaminergic medications may experience intense urges to gamble, increased sexual urges, binge eating, and other compulsive behaviors, which may resolve with dose reduction or discontinuation[6]\n\n### Population Subgroups\n\nThe choice between rechargeable and non‑rechargeable IPGs was significantly associated with affordability (χ²=19.13, p<0.001), and remote programming capability influenced brand selection.[10] Despite this, a preliminary survey of 17 DBS patients (85% of those approached) found that 76.5% used their patient programmer once per month or less, 17.7% never used it, and the most common function was checking battery life (58.8%).[11] Adaptive DBS shows promise, as one study reported that overall well‑being significantly improved with adaptive stimulation (p=0.007).[12] Caregivers of intermediate PD spent 6.6 hours per day caring, compared to 3.2 hours for early PD, and were less likely to be employed (25.3% vs 42.4%).[13] Personalized DBS programming using machine learning to optimize gait has produced meaningful improvements in walking, addressing a common post‑DBS challenge.[14] Remote patient monitoring devices led 79.9% of participants to agree that they improved mobility, and 48% perceived themselves as safer.[15] However, a meta‑synthesis of wearable device experiences identified barriers including discomfort, emotional distress, and interface difficulties that hinder long‑term adherence.[16] 87.3% of DBS patients used rechargeable IPGs (r-IPGs), 87.7% were satisfied with their device; among those with r-IPGs, 40.6% felt confident handling the device within one week of discharge, 71.4% recharged independently, and the mean recharge interval was 4.3 days.[10] Caregivers of people with advanced PD had significantly higher perceived burden (ZBI 34.5 vs 24.7 for early PD) and were more likely to take medication due to caregiving (34.7% vs 13.8%).[13] Assistive technologies also contribute: a scoping review reported that home automation significantly increased quality of life (p<0.001), and 63.5% of participants used speech‑to‑text functions to cope with tremors.[15]\n\n### Limitations\n\nLimitations: The pipeline's tier distribution reveals a heavy reliance on lower-quality evidence, with only 10% of sources classified as T1 primary studies and a striking 70% falling into the UNKNOWN tier. Six high-severity contradictions were detected, including a 972.4% relative difference in mortality estimates and a 481.0% divergence in hazard ratio, indicating sources disagree sharply on both magnitude and direction of key outcomes. The evidence horizon spans from 2010-01-01 to the present, but the predominance of unclassified sources and sparse representation in tiers T2 (1%), T3 (1%), and T5 (0%) further limit confidence in the synthesized findings.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 36a59dc009aa949f...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (clinical: domain_backends(clinical): {'europe_pmc': 27}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 478 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 262 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 6 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per clinical template. .\tests\fixtures\redaction_fixture_redact001.json:1160:      "report_text": "## Key Findings\n\n_Each finding below is a verbatim, span-verified statement carried up from the body section named in bold; citations are the body's._\n\n- **Technical_Standard.** ### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1]\n- **Legal_Framework.** 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2]\n- **Background.** The Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1]\n- **Key Findings.** In the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15]\n- **Evidence and Analysis.** The EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24]\n- **Comparative Assessment.** A key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9]\n\n### SAE J3016 driving-automation levels and the human-machine boundary\n\nLevel 5:\nFull Driving Automation.[1] Standard id: J3016_202104.[1] The SAE J3016_202104 standard defines six levels of driving automation.[1] Level 0 is No Driving Automation.[1] In contrast, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] Level 0 and Level 1 are No Driving Automation and Driver Assistance, respectively.[1] Finally, Level 5 is Full Driving Automation.[1] These are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation, respectively.[1] Although Level 0 is No Driving Automation, Level 1 is Driver Assistance.[1] In contrast, Level 2 is Partial Driving Automation, whereas Level 3 is Conditional Driving Automation.[1] The taxonomy's six levels are No Driving Automation, Driver Assistance, Partial Driving Automation, Conditional Driving Automation, High Driving Automation, and Full Driving Automation.[1]\n\n### UNECE WP.29 ALKS regulation (system-vs-driver responsibility)\n\nInstrument: UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS) is the instrument identified in the primary source.[2] The subject matter is Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2] In contrast, the regulation’s title clearly indicates its focus on automated lane keeping.[2] The regulation’s full designation is UN Regulation No. 157 - Automated Lane Keeping Systems (ALKS).[2] The regulation, UN Regulation No. 157, addresses Automated Lane Keeping Systems.[2]\n\n### NHTSA automated-driving-systems policy and crash-reporting order\n\nScope: Entities named in the General Order.[3] Issuing agency: NHTSA.[3] The scope of this order is precisely defined, applying exclusively to entities named in the General Order.[3] However, the reporting obligation is not triggered for every crash involving those entities; it is conditional upon specific temporal and outcome-based criteria.[3] Alternatively, the obligation arises if the crash involved a vulnerable road user being struck.[3] In contrast, a crash that does not meet these consequence thresholds, even if ADS was active within the 30-second window, would not require reporting under the order.[3] The reporting criteria also encompass an air bag deployment.[3] Finally, any individual being transported to a hospital for medical treatment as a result of the crash mandates a report.[3] By comparison, crashes where ADS was not in use within 30 seconds, or where the damage is below the specified property damage threshold, fall outside the mandatory reporting scope.[3]\n\n### Product-liability / negligence doctrine for ADAS manufacturers\n\nDefect categories: manufacturing defects; design defects; and inadequate instructions or warnings defects.[4]\n\n### Relevant ADAS / automated-feature crash-liability case law\n\nContract-bound content for adas_crash_case_law did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[5]\n\n### Proposed regulatory guidelines and liability-allocation recommendations\n\nContract-bound content for proposed_regulatory_guidelines did not survive strict verification against retrieved primary source text; this slot is a curator-actionable gap. See manifest.frame_coverage_report and human_gap_tasks.json for per-entity detail.[6]\n\n### Background\n\nThe Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE) International standard J3016_202104 defines six levels of driving automation, ranging from Level 0 (no driving automation) to Level 5 (full driving automation), and establishes a taxonomy for on-road motor vehicle driving automation systems.[1] Active safety systems such as electronic stability control (ESC) and automatic emergency braking (AEB) are excluded from this taxonomy because they provide only momentary intervention rather than sustained performance of the dynamic driving task (DDT).[1] Common ADAS functions include adaptive cruise control (ACC), blind spot detection (BSD), lane departure warning/lane keeping systems (LDW/LKS), intelligent headlamp control (IHC), and emergency brake assist (EBA).[7] Large-scale crash statistics indicate that among 7.7 million vehicles manufactured between 2015 and 2023, ADAS-equipped cars experienced on average a 27% reduction in forward-collision crashes and an 11% reduction in lane-departure crashes.[8] Blind Spot Monitoring (BSM) systems are estimated to reduce lane-change crashes by 14% and lane-change crashes with injuries by 23%.[9] For example, lane departure warning (LDW) systems were found to be turned off in 67.2% of observed vehicles due to lack of trust and excessive false alarms.[10] Camera-based DMS have been shown to detect driver distraction approximately 50 seconds sooner than steering-wheel-based DMS, and users of steering-wheel DMS were able to engage in 5.65 minutes of continuous distracted driving before an alert, compared to 2.25 minutes for camera-based DMS.[11] The increasing complexity of ADAS and automated driving systems has prompted the development of specialized accident investigation frameworks, such as a comprehensive system proposed in China that integrates traffic accident boundary analysis, control action safety analysis, and causal scene restoration.[12] To evaluate ADAS safety effectiveness, researchers have identified 94 influence parameters from sources including published literature, standardized tests, accident databases, and autonomous vehicle disengagement reports, with 77 parameters assigned importance levels through cluster analysis.[13] Driver drowsiness remains a significant safety concern, with the World Health Organization reporting approximately 1.35 million traffic-related deaths annually, and research indicating that steering wheel angle variability increases from 0.5–1.5° in alert drivers to 2–5° in fatigued states.[14] If the driver does not fully understand the capabilities and limitations of the system, this might lead to mode confusion and can cause surprises for the driver.[9] In the European Union, several SAE Level 0 ADAS will be mandatory by 2022, while no automated driving technology is yet permitted on the EU market.[9]\n\n### Key Findings\n\nIn the European Union, a 2025 European Parliament study advocates for a strict liability regime targeting high-risk AI systems, structured around a single responsible operator to avoid regulatory fragmentation among Member States.[15] This verdict signals a shift toward manufacturer accountability for AI driving systems.[16] NHTSA investigations have identified patterns of failures in Tesla's ADAS, including a probe of 2.4 million vehicles for crashes at intersections and emergency vehicle collisions, and a subsequent probe of 2.88 million vehicles involving 58 traffic violation reports, 14 crashes, and 23 injuries.[16] One analysis argues that semi-autonomous systems create a collaborative driving endeavor, and that automakers' marketing efforts to gain public trust may render them primarily liable when that trust is misplaced, with a reduction for the driver's comparative fault.[17] The opacity of AI decision-making and the 'problem of many hands'—diffusion of responsibility among multiple actors—create a liability gap that traditional tort frameworks struggle to address.[18] A growing body of policy analysis advocates for strict liability for high-risk AI systems, with proposals ranging from a single responsible operator under EU law to no-fault insurance schemes.[15].[18][19] One scholar proposes extending strict products liability to defective software components in autonomous vehicles, treating software creators as strictly liable for injuries caused by dangerously defective code.[20] A law and economics analysis suggests that negligence-based rules can efficiently accommodate AV-human interactions, but require substantial doctrinal reforms, including reconceptualization of fault standards for both AVs and human drivers.[21] Jurisdictional approaches vary: Michigan's product liability statutes govern claims involving defective ADAS, Louisiana applies a comparative fault system to apportion liability between driver and manufacturer, and the UK's Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 explicitly addresses liability for accidents resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[22][23][16]\n\n### Evidence and Analysis\n\nThe EU’s revised Product Liability Directive explicitly extends to software and AI-driven products, including autonomous vehicles, and introduces presumptions of defect and causation to ease the burden on injured parties.[15][24] In China, the undefined legal status of autonomous driving systems has prompted the development of a comprehensive accident investigation system that integrates boundary analysis, control action safety assessment, and causal scene restoration to allocate responsibility.[12] US legal scholarship argues that automakers’ marketing of ADAS features fosters driver trust, and when that trust is misplaced, products liability law supports primary manufacturer liability with a potential reduction for the driver’s comparative fault, rather than placing full responsibility on the human operator.[17] The revised PLD is an EU instrument for AI-related harm[15][24]\n\n### Comparative Assessment\n\nA key challenge across jurisdictions is allocating liability when semi-autonomous systems require constant human oversight, as drivers may over-rely on automation, leading to mode confusion and delayed re-engagement.[17][9] Under U.S. products liability law, automakers may face primary liability for ADAS failures, though the driver's comparative fault can reduce recovery[17][22] Legal scholars debate whether the human driver or the manufacturer bears responsibility when supervised autonomy fails in semi-autonomous vehicles.[17]\n\n### Implications\n\nIn the United Kingdom, the Automated and Electric Vehicles Act 2018 addresses liability for accidents caused by automated vehicles, including those resulting from unauthorized software alterations or failure to update software.[16] Texas has legislatively limited lessors’ liability for failing to retrofit optional safety systems, provided the vehicle met federal standards at purchase.[25] Empirical evidence from a survey of 260 drivers indicates that collision avoidance performance and system reliability are the strongest predictors of user trust, while road safety impact emerges as an independent societal benefit.[26] On the cost side, an Italian analysis estimates that an average ADAS investment of €1,629 per vehicle yields nationwide savings of €1.89 billion in 2024, with most systems becoming cost-effective within two to three years.[27] Daimler Trucks North America was granted summary judgment in a lawsuit.[25] However, the technology’s safety promise is tempered by the emergence of algorithmic negligence as a liability.[16]\n\n### Limitations\n\nFor driver monitoring systems, reported performance is highly protocol-dependent, and the lack of standardized benchmarks and cross-dataset validation makes cross-study accuracy comparisons misleading.[14] The shift from driver to vehicle control complicates liability attribution; evidence from aviation and early AV incidents suggests that human operators may become a “moral crumple zone,” blamed even when system design is a contributing factor.[19] The lack of integrated, context-rich data from connected vehicles hinders user-centered evaluation of ADAS; data are often siloed within engineering departments and not readily accessible to design teams.[28] The complexity of AV software, with estimates of up to 300 million lines of code, far exceeds that of aircraft, yet current algorithms often achieve only 95–98% reliability, whereas safety-critical functions may require 99.9999% reliability.[29]\n\nLimitations: The corpus is heavily skewed toward sources of unknown provenance, with 81% of documents lacking a tier classification and only 2% originating from T1 primary studies, limiting confidence in the evidence base. Two high-severity contradictions were detected: sources disagree on accuracy (relative difference 132.6%) and on ttr (relative difference 1342.0%), indicating substantial conflict in key metrics. The evidence horizon spans from 2015-01-01 to the present, which may exclude earlier foundational work.\n\n## Methods\nPre-registered protocol.json (SHA-256 268348acaf5d352c...).\nCorpus: Serper + Semantic Scholar + OpenAlex live retrieval, augmented by domain backends (policy: domain_backends(policy): {'serper_policy': 20}).\nRetrieval fetch outcome: 464 of 740 candidate sources fetched; 276 failed or timed out.\nGenerator model: deepseek/deepseek-v4-pro (multi-section: outline + 10 parallel sections + strict_verify + regen-on-failure).\nEvaluator model: google/gemma-4-31b-it (different family).\nSources classified using T1-T7 tier taxonomy.\nInclusion / exclusion per policy template.    10: claims and the must-cover slots gap-disclose — the recurring cause of
   11: `four_role_held` on clinical questions (#1188 + canary diagnosis).
   12: 
   13: This lane is an ENV-GATED, additive retry. For each must-cover entity that is
   14: STILL unsatisfied after the normal V30 frame fetch, it:
   15: 
   16: 1. Builds TARGETED safety queries (intervention anchor x entity safety term)
   17:    biased to an authoritative clinical-authority domain set UNION the entity's
   18:    OWN ``url_pattern`` host, and fires them through ``search_fn`` (Serper) to
   19:    DISCOVER candidate authoritative URLs.
   20: 2. FETCHES the discovered candidate URLs through the EXISTING live-retrieval
   21:    pipeline (``retrieval_fn`` = ``run_live_retrieval`` with ``seed_urls`` +
   22:    ``seed_only=True`` — the same AccessBypass/Zyte chokepoint, no Serper/S2
   23:    fan-out), producing canonical evidence rows that carry their REAL fetched
   24:    URLs.
   25: 3. RETURNS those evidence rows so the caller MERGES them into the corpus
   26:    (``evidence_for_gen``) — exactly like the saturation gap-round merge.
   27: 
   28: FAITHFULNESS (§-1.1 — LETHAL otherwise)
   29: =======================================
   30: The discovered rows are injected as ORDINARY corpus evidence carrying their REAL
   31: fetched URLs — they are NEVER keyed to an entity_id and NEVER relabeled with an
   32: entity's canonical ``url_pattern`` (the citation-faithfulness lie). All
   33: downstream gating is UNCHANGED: the generator can fill a must-cover slot ONLY if
   34: the new evidence passes the SAME strict_verify, and the 4-role coverage gate
   35: (`_entity_canonical_match`, operator-locked, NOT touched here) still requires
   36: EXACT canonical-identifier equality. An entity with no verifiable authoritative
   37: content stays the gap-disclosure it already was — no fabrication, no forced
   38: coverage.
   39: 
   40: HONEST SCOPE
   41: ============
   42: Because the 4-role coverage gate for url-pattern regulatory entities requires
   43: ``record.url == entity.url_pattern`` EXACTLY, injecting an ALTERNATE authoritative
   44: URL CANNOT flip that specific entity's coverage. What this lane does is get the
   45: authoritative SAFETY CONTENT into the corpus so the generator can write VERIFIED
   46: contraindication / dosing claims (directly addressing #1190's "absent from the
   47: cited research corpus"). It flips 4-role coverage only for DOI/PMID-keyed
   48: entities, or when the entity's exact ``url_pattern`` is itself among the fetched
   49: URLs — not for the url-pattern regulatory entity class as a rule.
   50: 
   51: PURITY / TESTABILITY
   52: ====================
   53: ``run_required_entity_lane`` takes the search and retrieval callables by
   54: DEPENDENCY INJECTION (``search_fn`` / ``retrieval_fn``). Production wires the
   55: real Serper search and ``run_live_retrieval``; tests pass deterministic stubs.
   56: NO network in this module. The caller owns the env gate and the corpus merge.
   57: """

   68: logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

   70: # --- env gate (HARD constraint 1: flag-OFF = byte-identical) ------------------
   71: _LANE_ENABLED_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_RETRIEVAL"
   72: 
   73: # --- authoritative-domain set (LAW VI: named const, env-overridable) ----------
   74: # The operator-approved clinical-authority set. Each entity's OWN url_pattern
   75: # host is UNIONed onto this per-entity so url-only-canonical entities
   76: # (ods.od.nih.gov, accessdata.fda.gov, health-products.canada.ca) are reachable
   77: # in the targeted search (the default list alone does not cover them).
   78: _REQUIRED_ENTITY_DOMAINS_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_DOMAINS"
   79: _DEFAULT_REQUIRED_ENTITY_DOMAINS: tuple[str, ...] = (
   80:     "fda.gov",
   81:     "dailymed.nlm.nih.gov",
   82:     "ema.europa.eu",
   83:     "nice.org.uk",
   84:     "who.int",
   85:     "drugs.com",
   86: )
   87: 
   88: # --- bounds (HARD constraint 3: named consts, env-overridable, no runaway) ----
   89: # Max distinct targeted queries fired per unsatisfied entity.
   90: _MAX_QUERIES_PER_ENTITY_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_MAX_QUERIES_PER_ENTITY"
   91: _DEFAULT_MAX_QUERIES_PER_ENTITY = 3
   92: # Max unsatisfied entities the lane will process in one run.
   93: _MAX_ENTITIES_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_MAX_ENTITIES"
   94: _DEFAULT_MAX_ENTITIES = 12
   95: # Max search results requested per targeted query (discovery width).
   96: _MAX_RESULTS_PER_QUERY_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_MAX_RESULTS_PER_QUERY"
   97: _DEFAULT_MAX_RESULTS_PER_QUERY = 5
   98: # Max candidate URLs FETCHED per entity (the billed fetch cap for this lane).
   99: _MAX_SEED_URLS_PER_ENTITY_ENV = "PG_REQUIRED_ENTITY_MAX_SEED_URLS_PER_ENTITY"
  100: _DEFAULT_MAX_SEED_URLS_PER_ENTITY = 3
  101: 
  102: # --- minimum verifiable span (mirrors contract_section_runner default) --------
  103: # A METADATA_ONLY row whose direct_quote is shorter than this has no verifiable
  104: # span to cite — it is treated as UNSATISFIED (the same floor the gap-routing in
  105: # contract_section_runner.py:370-386 uses). Env-overridable (LAW VI).
  106: _MIN_VERIFIABLE_SPAN_ENV = "PG_MIN_VERIFIABLE_SPAN_CHARS"
  107: _DEFAULT_MIN_VERIFIABLE_SPAN_CHARS = 50
  108: 
  109: # --- safety-term phrasings per S0 category (deterministic, no magic strings) --
  110: # Maps an entity's s0_category -> the safety phrasing appended to the
  111: # intervention anchor to build a targeted query. A category absent here falls
  112: # back to the generic safety terms below (still bounded).
  113: _S0_CATEGORY_QUERY_TERMS: dict[str, tuple[str, ...]] = {
  114:     "contraindications": ("contraindications", "who should not take", "warnings"),
  115:     "dosing_limits": ("dosing", "maximum dose", "tolerable upper intake limit"),
  116:     "black_box_warnings": ("boxed warning", "black box warning", "safety"),
  117:     "regulatory_status": ("label", "approval status", "prescribing information"),
  118: }
  119: _GENERIC_SAFETY_TERMS: tuple[str, ...] = (
  120:     "safety adverse effects",
  121:     "contraindications",
  122:     "dosing",
  123: )
  124: 
  125: # Caller-supplied source/origin labels so the merged rows are honestly tagged
  126: # as required-entity-lane discoveries (not mislabeled as primary-trial seeds).
  127: SEED_SOURCE_LABEL = "required_entity_lane"
  128: SEED_QUERY_ORIGIN = "required_entity_targeted_search"

  131: @dataclass
  132: class RequiredEntityLaneResult:
  133:     """Outcome of one lane invocation (for the manifest / audit trail).
  134: 
  135:     ``evidence_rows`` are the NEWLY fetched corpus rows (real fetched URLs) the
  285:     if isinstance(hit, str):
  286:         return hit.strip()
  287:     if isinstance(hit, Mapping):
  288:         for key in ("url", "link"):
  289:             value = hit.get(key)
  290:             if isinstance(value, str) and value.strip():
  291:                 return value.strip()
  292:     return ""

  294: 
  295: def run_required_entity_lane(
  296:     *,
  297:     frame_rows: Sequence[FrameRow],
  298:     entity_meta_by_id: Mapping[str, Any],
  299:     entity_cfg_by_id: Mapping[str, Mapping[str, Any]],
  300:     research_question: str,
  301:     scope_overrides: Optional[Mapping[str, Any]],
  302:     search_fn: Callable[..., Any],
  303:     retrieval_fn: Callable[..., Any],
  304: ) -> RequiredEntityLaneResult:
  305:     """Run the targeted required-entity lane (ENV-GATED by the CALLER).
  306: 
  307:     For each UNSATISFIED FrameRow:
  308:       1. Build + fire targeted authoritative-domain-biased search queries via
  309:          ``search_fn(query, domains=, max_results=)`` and COLLECT the candidate
  310:          URLs (capped per-entity and total-entities).
  311:       2. FETCH the collected candidate URLs through ``retrieval_fn`` (the live
  312:          retriever) with ``seed_urls`` + ``seed_only=True`` — same Zyte
  313:          chokepoint, no Serper/S2 fan-out — producing canonical evidence rows
  314:          carrying their REAL fetched URLs.
  315: 
  316:     Returns the NEWLY fetched evidence rows for the CALLER to merge into the
  317:     corpus (dedup + evidence_id renumber, like the saturation gap-round). The
  318:     lane NEVER mutates ``frame_rows`` and NEVER keys a row to an entity_id, so a
  319:     fetched row can never be relabeled as the entity (provenance honesty).
  320: 
  321:     DEPENDENCY INJECTION: ``search_fn`` and ``retrieval_fn`` are injected so this
  322:     is pure / offline-testable. The caller owns the env gate (``lane_enabled()``).
  323:     """
  324:     max_entities = _bounded_int_env(_MAX_ENTITIES_ENV, _DEFAULT_MAX_ENTITIES)
  325:     max_results = _bounded_int_env(
  326:         _MAX_RESULTS_PER_QUERY_ENV, _DEFAULT_MAX_RESULTS_PER_QUERY
  327:     )
  328:     max_seed_urls = _bounded_int_env(
  329:         _MAX_SEED_URLS_PER_ENTITY_ENV, _DEFAULT_MAX_SEED_URLS_PER_ENTITY
  330:     )
  331:     floor = _min_verifiable_span_chars()
  332: 
  333:     attempted: list[str] = []
  334:     queries_by_entity: dict[str, tuple[str, ...]] = {}
  335:     seed_urls: list[str] = []
  336:     seen_seed: set[str] = set()
  337: 
  338:     for row in frame_rows:
  339:         if len(attempted) >= max_entities:
  340:             break
  341:         if not frame_row_is_unsatisfied(row, min_span_chars=floor):
  342:             continue
  343:         entity_id = row.entity_id
  344:         entity_cfg = entity_cfg_by_id.get(entity_id)
  345:         if entity_cfg is None:
  346:             # No config to anchor the targeted query on — cannot act; skip.
  347:             continue
  348:         attempted.append(entity_id)
  349: 
  350:         anchor = _intervention_anchor(
  351:             entity_meta=entity_meta_by_id.get(entity_id),
  352:             scope_overrides=scope_overrides,
  353:             research_question=research_question,
  354:         )
  355:         queries = build_targeted_queries(
  356:             entity=entity_cfg, intervention_anchor=anchor
  357:         )
  358:         queries_by_entity[entity_id] = queries
  359: 
  360:         # (1) Targeted authoritative-domain-biased search -> DISCOVER candidate
  361:         # URLs (consume the search output). Errors are non-fatal: a failed query
  362:         # simply yields no candidates for that entity.
  363:         domains = _domains_for_entity(entity_cfg)
  364:         entity_urls: list[str] = []
  365:         for query in queries:
  366:             try:
  367:                 hits = search_fn(query, domains=domains, max_results=max_results)
  368:             except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — discovery is best-effort
  369:                 logger.warning(
  370:                     "required_entity_lane: search failed for entity=%s query=%r",
  371:                     entity_id,
  372:                     query,
  373:                 )
  374:                 continue
  375:             for hit in hits or []:
  376:                 url = _result_url(hit)
  377:                 if not url or url in seen_seed:
  378:                     continue
  379:                 seen_seed.add(url)
  380:                 entity_urls.append(url)
  381:                 if len(entity_urls) >= max_seed_urls:
  382:                     break
  383:             if len(entity_urls) >= max_seed_urls:
  384:                 break
  385:         seed_urls.extend(entity_urls)
  386: 
  387:     if not seed_urls:
  388:         return RequiredEntityLaneResult(
  389:             evidence_rows=[],
  390:             attempted_entity_ids=tuple(attempted),
  391:             queries_by_entity=queries_by_entity,
  392:             seed_urls=(),
  393:         )
  394: 
  395:     # (2) FETCH the discovered URLs through the existing live retriever, seed-only
  396:     # (no Serper/S2 fan-out), so each candidate is fetched + classified through
  397:     # the SAME chokepoint and emerges as a canonical evidence row with its REAL
  398:     # url. A retrieval failure leaves the corpus unchanged (best-effort).
  399:     try:
  400:         result = retrieval_fn(
  401:             research_question=research_question,
  402:             amplified_queries=[],
  403:             seed_urls=list(seed_urls),
  404:             seed_only=True,
  405:             seed_source=SEED_SOURCE_LABEL,
  406:             seed_query_origin=SEED_QUERY_ORIGIN,
  407:             anchor_seed=False,
  408:         )
  409:     except Exception:  # noqa: BLE001 — a failed fetch leaves the corpus as-is
  410:         logger.warning(
  411:             "required_entity_lane: live-retrieval fetch raised for %d seed urls; "
  412:             "leaving corpus unchanged",
  413:             len(seed_urls),
  414:         )
  415:         return RequiredEntityLaneResult(
  416:             evidence_rows=[],
  417:             attempted_entity_ids=tuple(attempted),
  418:             queries_by_entity=queries_by_entity,
  419:             seed_urls=tuple(seed_urls),
  420:         )
  421: 
  422:     evidence_rows = list(getattr(result, "evidence_rows", []) or [])
  423:     return RequiredEntityLaneResult(
  424:         evidence_rows=evidence_rows,
  425:         attempted_entity_ids=tuple(attempted),
  426:         queries_by_entity=queries_by_entity,
  427:         seed_urls=tuple(seed_urls),
  428:     )

 succeeded in 8255ms:

  217: def load_required_entities(template: dict, slug: str) -> list[dict]:

  226:             f"load_required_entities: no {_KEY_REPORT_CONTRACT}[{slug!r}] in the scope "
  227:             f"template; the native required-element denominator must exist (fail-closed)."

  232:             f"load_required_entities: {_KEY_REPORT_CONTRACT}[{slug!r}].{_KEY_REQUIRED_ENTITIES} "
  233:             f"is missing or empty; cannot build a coverage denominator (fail-closed)."
  234:         )

  294: def _entity_canonical_match(entity: dict, record: Mapping[str, Any]) -> bool:
  295:     """EXACT canonical-identifier match (P2 #4): DOI / PMID / full canonical URL.
  296: 
  297:     Equality per (entity_key, record_key) pair only — never substring. The URL pair compares
  298:     the entity's `url_pattern` against the record's resolved `url`; a broad `url_pattern`
  299:     FRAGMENT can never equal a full resolved URL, so it fails closed.
  300:     """
  301:     for entity_key, record_key in _CANONICAL_MATCH_PAIRS:
  302:         entity_value = entity.get(entity_key)
  303:         if entity_value in (None, ""):
  304:             continue
  305:         record_value = record.get(record_key)
  306:         if record_value in (None, ""):
  307:             continue
  308:         if str(entity_value).strip() == str(record_value).strip():
  309:             return True
  310:     return False
  311:

  313: # --- I-perm-002 (#1196): semantic contraindication credit (default OFF -> literal-exact) -----
  314: # The CDC contraindication source for `probiotic_immunocompromised_contraindication` says probiotics
  315: # "should be avoided for patients who ... are immunocompromised" — it NEVER uses the word
  505:     Thin wrapper over `_content_requirements_satisfied_impl` that takes the semantic decision from the
  506:     `PG_SWEEP_SEMANTIC_CONTRAINDICATION` env flag (default OFF -> byte-identical literal-exact match).
  507:     Behavior is unchanged from the pre-refactor function: OFF reads identical, ON relaxes ONLY the
  508:     contraindication concept token to a negation-guarded direction phrase.
  509:     """
  510:     return _content_requirements_satisfied_impl(
  511:         claim_text, entity, semantic=_semantic_contraindication_enabled()
  512:     )

  515: def _claim_covers_entity(
  516:     claim_evidence_records: list[Mapping[str, Any]],
  517:     claim_text: str,
  518:     entity: dict,
  519:     *,
  520:     is_s0: bool,
  521: ) -> bool:
  522:     """Coverage test (§7.D, Codex-tightened + P2 #4).
  523: 
  524:     ENTITY coverage: True iff the claim cites evidence whose CANONICAL identifier EXACTLY
  525:     matches the entity's. anchor-string-in-sentence alone does NOT grant coverage.
  526: 
  527:     S0 SAFETY-CATEGORY coverage (stricter): an S0 entity is covered ONLY when BOTH (a) the
  528:     canonical evidence match holds AND (b) the claim text deterministically satisfies the
  529:     entity's `coverage_content_requirements`. A broad source citation without the content
  530:     match does NOT satisfy an S0 safety category.
  531:     """
  532:     if not any(_entity_canonical_match(entity, rec) for rec in claim_evidence_records):
  533:         return False
  534:     if is_s0 and not _content_requirements_satisfied(claim_text, entity):
  535:         return False
  536:     return True
  537: 
  538: 
  539: # FX-03 / I-ready-017 (#1107): the 4-role seam (Sentinel decomposition + Judge) joins each
  540: # EvidenceDocument.text as the SPAN the claim is checked against. Pre-FX-03 the WHOLE record text
  541: # was passed, so a claim could be graded VERIFIED on support living ANYWHERE in the document, not
  542: # in the cited [start:end] window (BUG-02: confirmed out-of-span false-accept, claim 06-004).
  543: _GATE_B_CITED_SPAN_ENV = "PG_GATE_B_CITED_SPAN"
  544: _GATE_B_SPAN_WINDOW_ENV = "PG_GATE_B_SPAN_WINDOW_BYTES"
  545: _GATE_B_SPAN_WINDOW_DEFAULT = 400
  640:                 f"cited token must resolve to an evidence record (fail-closed)."
  641:             )
  642:         text = str(record.get(_RECORD_TEXT_KEY) or "").strip()
  643:         if not text:
  644:             raise ValueError(
  645:                 f"build_native_gate_b_inputs: evidence_id {evidence_id!r} has empty "
  646:                 f"evidence text; cannot evaluate a claim against empty evidence (fail-closed)."
  647:             )
  648:         documents.append(
  649:             EvidenceDocument(
  650:                 doc_id=evidence_id,
  651:                 text=_normalize_span_text(_cited_window_text(text, token)),
  652:             )
  653:         )
  654:         records.append(record)
  655:     return documents, records

  658: def build_native_gate_b_inputs(
  659:     *,
  660:     multi: Any,
  661:     template: dict,
  662:     slug: str,
  663:     domain: str,
  664:     evidence_lookup: Mapping[str, Mapping[str, Any]],
  665:     model_slugs: dict[str, str],
  666:     d8_config: D8PolicyConfig,
  667: ) -> NativeGateBBundle:
  668:     """Build NATIVE 4-role Gate-B inputs from a finished report + native scope contract.
  669: 
  670:     Pure function (no I/O, no network). Returns a `NativeGateBBundle` (inputs + audit_map);
  671:     M3b persists the audit map. NEVER reads `outputs/dr_benchmark/`. `domain` is carried for
  672:     the audit trail / caller symmetry; the denominator comes from `template[slug]`.
  673:     """
  674:     entities = load_required_entities(template, slug)
  675:     required_element_ids = [entity[_KEY_ENTITY_ID] for entity in entities]
  676: 
  677:     # Validate every entity up front (fail-closed); cache (severity, s0_category) per entity.
  678:     validated: list[tuple[dict, str, str | None]] = []
  679:     required_s0_categories_set: set[str] = set()
  680:     for entity in entities:
  681:         severity, s0_category = validate_entity_severity(entity, d8_config)
  682:         validated.append((entity, severity, s0_category))
  683:         if s0_category is not None:
  684:             required_s0_categories_set.add(s0_category)
  685: 
  686:     claims: list[FourRoleClaim] = []
  687:     audit_map: dict[str, dict] = {}
  688:     for section_index, section in enumerate(multi.sections):
  689:         kept = getattr(section, "kept_sentences_pre_resolve", []) or []
  690:         sentence_index = -1
  691:         for verification in kept:
  692:             if not verification.is_verified:
  693:                 continue
  694:             sentence_index += 1
  695:             sentence = verification.sentence
  696:             normalized = _normalize_sentence(sentence)
  697:             digest = hashlib.sha256(normalized.encode("utf-8")).hexdigest()[:_CLAIM_HASH_HEX_LEN]
  698:             claim_id = f"{section_index:02d}-{sentence_index:03d}-{digest}"
  699: 
  700:             evidence_ids = [token.evidence_id for token in verification.tokens]
  701:             # FX-03 (#1107): pass the TOKENS (carrying start/end), not just the ids — _resolve_evidence
  702:             # slices each EvidenceDocument.text to the cited bounded window so the seam judges the
  703:             # claim against the cited SPAN, not the whole source doc (BUG-02 out-of-span false-accept).
  704:             # evidence_ids is retained above for the audit_map trail.
  705:             documents, records = _resolve_evidence(verification.tokens, evidence_lookup)
  706: 
  707:             covered_element_ids: list[str] = []
  708:             covered_s0_categories: list[str] = []
  709:             best_rank = _SEVERITY_RANK[_DEFAULT_OBSERVE_ONLY_SEVERITY]
  710:             for entity, severity, s0_category in validated:
  711:                 is_s0 = severity == _SEVERITY_S0
  712:                 if not _claim_covers_entity(records, sentence, entity, is_s0=is_s0):
  713:                     continue
  714:                 covered_element_ids.append(entity[_KEY_ENTITY_ID])
  715:                 best_rank = max(best_rank, _SEVERITY_RANK[severity])
  716:                 if is_s0 and s0_category is not None:
  717:                     covered_s0_categories.append(s0_category)
  718: 
  719:             # I-perm-020 (#1212): S0 coverage binder. AFTER per-claim verification (these are the
  720:             # strict_verify-VERIFIED sentences, `verification.is_verified` is the loop guard above),
  721:             # credit a required S0 SAFETY category whose entity this VERIFIED claim satisfies via the
  722:             # FULL I-perm-002 conjunction (canonical evidence match + population anchor +
  723:             # contraindication DIRECTION + negation guard, semantic recognition forced ON inside the
  724:             # binder). Fixes the measured drb_76 false hold (a faithful "not recommended for the
  725:             # immunocompromised" contraindication that the literal-token era left un-credited). DEFAULT
  726:             # OFF: the `PG_S0_COVERAGE_BINDER` flag is read at CALL TIME here (never at import); when OFF
  727:             # the binder module is NEVER imported and NEVER called, so `covered_s0_categories` /
  728:             # `covered_element_ids` / `best_rank` are byte-identical to legacy. The credit is an
  729:             # idempotent set-UNION (harmless if the existing literal path or
  730:             # PG_SWEEP_SEMANTIC_CONTRAINDICATION already credited the same category) and is STILL gated
  731:             # downstream by the 4-role `verdict==VERIFIED` check in release_policy — additive credit, NOT
  732:             # a gate relaxation. The D8 threshold and the release rule are untouched. A bound element is
  733:             # an S0 entity by construction, so it also raises `best_rank` to S0 (a claim that earns an S0
  734:             # category is an S0 claim — keeping severity coherent with s0_categories, exactly as the
  735:             # native loop above promotes severity when it covers an S0 entity).
  736:             if _s0_coverage_binder_enabled():
  737:                 from src.polaris_graph.roles.coverage_binder import bind_s0_coverage
  738: 
  739:                 bound_categories, bound_element_ids = bind_s0_coverage(
  740:                     claim_text=sentence,
  741:                     claim_evidence_records=records,
  742:                     validated_entities=validated,
  743:                 )
  744:                 for category in bound_categories:
  745:                     if category not in covered_s0_categories:
  746:                         covered_s0_categories.append(category)
  747:                 for element_id in bound_element_ids:
  748:                     if element_id not in covered_element_ids:
  749:                         covered_element_ids.append(element_id)
  750:                 if bound_element_ids:
  751:                     best_rank = max(best_rank, _SEVERITY_RANK[_SEVERITY_S0])
  752: 
  753:             claim_severity = next(
  754:                 sev for sev, rank in _SEVERITY_RANK.items() if rank == best_rank
  755:             )
  756:             s0_categories = sorted(set(covered_s0_categories))
  757: 
  758:             claims.append(
  759:                 FourRoleClaim(
  760:                     claim_id=claim_id,
  761:                     claim_text=sentence,
  762:                     evidence_documents=documents,
  763:                     severity=claim_severity,
  764:                     s0_categories=s0_categories,
  765:                     covered_element_ids=covered_element_ids,
  766:                 )
  767:             )
  768:             audit_map[claim_id] = {
  769:                 "section_index": section_index,
  770:                 "section_title": getattr(section, "title", ""),
  771:                 "sentence": sentence,
  772:                 "evidence_ids": evidence_ids,
  773:                 "covered_element_ids": covered_element_ids,
  774:                 "severity": claim_severity,
  775:                 "s0_categories": s0_categories,
  776:             }
  777: 
  778:     if not claims:
  779:         raise ValueError(
  780:             "build_native_gate_b_inputs: zero kept (verified) sentences; a 4-role "
  781:             "evaluation over no claims cannot produce a release decision (fail-closed, "
  782:             "no vacuous input)."
  783:         )
  784: 
  785:     inputs = FourRoleEvaluationInputs(
  786:         claims=claims,
  787:         coverage_ledger=CoverageLedger(required_element_ids=required_element_ids),
  788:         required_s0_categories=sorted(required_s0_categories_set),
  789:         model_slugs=model_slugs,
  790:         rewrite_already_attempted=False,

 2320: async def _run_section(
 2321:     section: SectionPlan,

 2344:     function injects per-section CROSS-TRIAL SYNTHESIS suggestions

 2346:     run-12 verdict: V31+V32 lifted slot quality but Narrative depth
 2347:     stayed LB because Efficacy + Mechanism were slot-stacked. M-72

 2352:     ev_subset = [

 2354:         if ev_id in evidence_pool
 2355:     ]

 2360:         # `error="no_evidence_in_pool"` is preserved for telemetry so the cause stays auditable.

 2362:             title=section.title, focus=section.focus,

 4695:                 for f, v, u in candidates
 4696:             ],
 4697:             "matched_fields": [
 4698:                 {"field": f, "value": v} for f, v in matched
 4699:             ],
 4700:             "match_count": len(matched),

 7165:                 return _seam_parent_ctx.run(_run_under_ctx)
 7166: 
 7167:             _seam_held_reason = None
 7168:             # Manage the executor MANUALLY (NOT `with`): a `with ThreadPoolExecutor` __exit__ calls
 7169:             # shutdown(wait=True), which BLOCKS until the wedged worker finishes — defeating the
 7170:             # timeout entirely (Codex diff-gate P1). shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True) returns
 7171:             # promptly so the held manifest is written AT PG_FOUR_ROLE_SEAM_TIMEOUT_SECONDS; the
 7172:             # orphaned worker thread exits on its own per-call timeout. Mirrors audit_ir/parallel_fetch.py.
 7173:             _hb("four_role_started")  # I-obs-001 #1141 AC1: bracket the up-to-7200s verifier phase
 7174:             _seam_pool = _seam_futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=1)
 7175:             try:
 7176:                 four_role_result = _seam_pool.submit(_seam_worker).result(
 7177:                     timeout=_seam_timeout
 7178:                 )
 7179:             except _seam_futures.TimeoutError:
 7180:                 _seam_held_reason = "seam_timeout"
 7181:             except _SeamBudgetExceededError:
 7182:                 # A verifier cap breach (PG_MAX_COST_PER_RUN) MUST propagate to the existing outer
 7183:                 # abort_budget_exceeded handler (the clean budget-abort contract) — NOT be swallowed
 7184:                 # into a held seam_error (Codex diff-gate iter-2 P1). The worker's `finally` already
 7185:                 # updated the cost holder, so the `finally` below reconciles the full spend before
 7186:                 # this re-raises.
 7187:                 raise
 7188:             except Exception as _seam_exc:  # noqa: BLE001 - any OTHER seam failure must fail CLOSED
 7189:                 _seam_held_reason = f"seam_error:{type(_seam_exc).__name__}:{str(_seam_exc)[:120]}"
 7190:             finally:
 7191:                 # Codex AC1-gate iter-2 P1-1: clear the live flag UNDER _hb_lock so it is mutually
 7192:                 # exclusive with the worker's check-then-write in _hb_claims. An in-flight callback
 7193:                 # either completes its write BEFORE this acquires the lock, or is blocked and then sees
 7194:                 # the cleared flag and returns — so NO four_role_progress write can land after this
 7195:                 # point. Stops an orphaned post-timeout worker from overwriting the terminal heartbeat
 7196:                 # or bleeding stale progress into the next query's state/run_status.json.
 7197:                 with _hb_lock:
 7198:                     _seam_hb_live[0] = False
 7199:                 # NON-BLOCKING shutdown so a wedged worker cannot delay the held manifest (P1).
 7200:                 _seam_pool.shutdown(wait=False, cancel_futures=True)
 7201:                 # Budget reconciliation: on the SUCCESS path the worker's `finally` already updated
 7202:                 # `_seam_cost_holder`, so this write-back is EXACT. On the TIMEOUT path the worker is
 7203:                 # still running (holder == parent cost -> delta 0), so the in-flight verifier spend is
 7204:                 # NOT added to the parent cap — an acceptable tradeoff (Codex P2): the run is ABORTING
 7205:                 # (release HELD), the operator authorized spend, the orphaned worker's cost is bounded
